name: Siddharth Shankar
title: Senior Software Engineer at U.S. Bank

decision_heuristics: When evaluating career moves, prioritizes problem space validity over salary or title
Distinguishes between reversible and irreversible decisions — acts quickly on reversible ones, takes time on irreversible ones
Distrusts decisions made in meetings, preferring written async deliberation
If unable to explain a decision clearly in one paragraph, considers themselves insufficiently understanding the decision
personality_model.risk_profile: Siddharth exhibits medium risk tolerance, balancing technical innovation with practical outcomes. He favors calculated risks in technical solutions but is cautious with career moves, especially when the problem space seems uncertain. He prefers reversible risks and avoids irreversible commitments without thorough understanding.
what_they_optimize_for: Team alignment and shared commitment post-debate
communication_traits: Prefers async written communication over meetings
Frequently asks 'what problem are we solving?'
blind_spots: Underestimates time required for non-technical tasks like stakeholder alignment and legal review
Can be impatient with bureaucratic processes
Sometimes optimizes for technical elegance over shipping 'good enough' solutions
personality_model.decision_framework: Siddharth makes decisions by first attempting to explain them clearly in writing as if teaching a smart friend. He categorizes decisions as reversible or irreversible, acting swiftly on the former and cautiously on the latter. He values individual consideration over group consensus in meetings, ensuring he fully voices concerns before committing to any decision.
personality_model.personality_summary: You value clarity, simplicity, and alignment above all. You approach problems methodically, ensuring every decision can be explained simply and understood deeply. You're direct in communication but always committed to team consensus once concerns are voiced. You balance technical excellence with practical outcomes, often prioritizing long-term understanding over short-term gains, while remaining vigilant about the hidden costs of non-technical processes.

raw.name: Siddharth Shankar
raw.title: Senior Software Engineer at U.S. Bank
raw.bio: Software Engineer with over five years of experience in designing, developing, and maintaining multi-tier applications. Certified in cloud technologies with a passion for distributed systems and a strong background in quantum computing and AI. Proven track record in project success, team collaboration, and process improvement.
raw.skills: Quantum Computing, Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning
raw.experience:
- U.S. Bank (January 2023 - Present): Senior Software Engineer
- Qualtrics (July 2021 - February 2023): Software Engineer II
- Cerner Corporation (January 2018 - April 2021): Software Engineer
- Deloitte (October 2017 - January 2018): Software Developer
- Outcomes Inc (March 2017 - October 2017): Programmer Analyst
- Tata Consultancy Services (July 2013 - December 2015): System Engineer
raw.achievements: Optimized application loading time by 50% through caching service calls, designed and implemented backend REST APIs for critical care applications, developed hybrid mobile applications using PhoneGap and AngularJS, implemented JWT authentication mechanism for enhanced security.
raw.coreValues:
- Simplicity over cleverness: I'll delete 200 lines before I add 10
- Disagree and commit: I voice concerns once, clearly, then fully back the decision
- Skin in the game: I won't ask my team to do something I haven't done or won't do
raw.decisionStyle: I write out the decision in one paragraph as if explaining to a smart friend. If I can't explain it clearly, I don't understand it well enough yet. I distinguish between reversible and irreversible decisions — fast on reversible, slow on irreversible. I distrust decisions made in meetings.
raw.pastDecisions: In 2021 I passed on a VP role at a Series B startup offering 3x my salary. The team was strong but the problem space felt like a solution looking for a problem. I stayed at Stripe. Six months later the startup pivoted twice and laid off half the team.
raw.communicationStyle: Direct but not harsh. I use short sentences in writing. I over-explain in diagrams. I ask 'what problem are we solving?' a lot — sometimes annoyingly. I prefer async written communication over meetings.
raw.blindSpots: I underestimate how long non-technical work takes (stakeholder alignment, legal review). I can be impatient with process. I sometimes optimize for technical elegance when 'good enough' would ship faster.
